title: Whittled beech kitchen peg
subtitle: A seasoned-wood clothes peg shaped for the kitchen drying rack
excerpt: A beech clothes peg cut and whittled from a short seasoned length: the body, two arms, and a slot that holds fabric under spring pressure using a rubber band. No lathe, no router, no spring mechanism to buy. A project that takes an hour and produces something used daily.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the hand-cut slot method; traditional domestic peg construction from Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content for indoor use.
Blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short vertical cut into the wood that stops the next push or pull cut, preventing the grain from tearing past the intended boundary.
A general-purpose Scandinavian carving knife with a straight blade 7 to 10 cm long; the standard tool for whittling and spoon carving.

content.text: A clothes peg is a two-part pinching mechanism: two arms connected at the head and opened by a rubber band looped around both. The kitchen version; slightly shorter arms, slightly wider slot; is made from a single short length of
seasoned wood
cut in half lengthways after shaping. Beech is the traditional peg wood: hard, close-grained, and resistant to the moisture of a kitchen. Ash works equally well.
The wood

A piece of kiln-dried beech: 12 cm long, 2 cm square in section. A beech dowel of 2 cm diameter works identically. The grain should run straight along the length; a knot anywhere in the piece will make the split unpredictable. Ash is the alternative: slightly lighter, equally moisture-resistant.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
for the main shaping, and a marking knife for the slot definition. A rubber band (size 65 or 33) for the spring mechanism. No other tools are needed.
Method
Use
push cuts
throughout the shaping. The blank is small; keep the blade-path well clear of the fingers at all times. Strop every 15 minutes.

Shape the head: round off one end of the blank to a domed profile, 1.5 cm deep from the flat end-grain face. This is the gripping head of the peg. Push cuts along the grain remove the corners; thumb-pivot cuts refine the dome.

Shape the arm section: chamfer the two long edges of the arm section (the remaining 10 cm of the blank) so the cross-section is an octagon rather than a square. This removes the sharp corners that would mark fabric.
Cut the slot: use the marking knife to cut a
stop cut

at 8 cm from the head end, perpendicular to the grain. Then carefully split the blank from the far end toward this stop cut along the centre grain line. The split should run exactly along the centre; if it drifts, use the sloyd knife as a wedge on the other side to steer it back. The stop cut prevents the split from running past the arm-head boundary.

Taper the inside faces of the split arms: a few push cuts on each inner face, creating a slight inward taper toward the tip. This gives the peg more grip when the rubber band is looped around both arms.

Loop a rubber band around both arms at the head end. The rubber band serves as the spring; the arms close under its tension. Test by pinching the arm tips open: they should spring closed when released with enough force to grip a teatowel.
Finishing

Leave unfinished or sand through 120 grit and apply a light coat of boiled linseed oil (not raw; the peg is not food-contact). Boiled linseed oil sets in 24 hours and keeps the wood from picking up kitchen moisture. Remove and replace the rubber band when it perishes; usually every 6 to 12 months in kitchen use.
Variations
Ash: lighter and slightly less hard than beech; works identically.
Garden peg: start with a 16 cm blank, shape the arm tips to a slight wedge for gripping thicker fabric.
Care
Keep dry when not in use. Replace the rubber band when it perishes. The peg itself will last for years if kept out of standing water.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the kitchen peg.

items.symptom: The split drifts to one side during splitting
items.cause: The grain is not perfectly straight, or the splitting force is unevenly applied
items.fix: Use the sloyd knife as a lever in the split: press against the thicker side to steer the split back toward centre. Work in small increments.

items.symptom: Arms too stiff to open easily
items.cause: Head section too thick relative to arm length
items.fix: Thin the inside faces of the arms slightly with push cuts to reduce the closing pressure.

items.cause: Arm tips too wide or inside faces not tapered
items.fix: Taper the inside faces more steeply toward the tip. A 1 mm taper per 5 cm of arm length is a starting point.
